sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 23, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Critical finding from transition trace: - UP/DOWN moves both squares together, but if either is blocked, NEITHER moves - LEFT moves squares apart (left←, right→), RIGHT moves them together (left→, right←) - Left square at rows 39-43, right at rows 44-48 - Right square blocked by orange region (rows 24-43), can only move in rows 44-55 - Left square can move in rows 9-55 Let me try SPACE and also explore horizontal movement to find the goal.
- Goal model: Need to figure out target positions for sky blue squares. Let me try MOUSE to interact with specific elements.
- Plan: Probe with a simple directional action to see what moves and how the game responds.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 22 of about 30 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see:
- Left square: cols 9-13 (moved from 14-18, so it moved left by 5)
- Right square: cols 44-48 (stayed at same position)
Wait, the right square stayed at cols 44-48. But the animation shows 50 cells changed (2 squares × 25 cells each). Let me check if the right square actually moved.
Looking at the row 54 string: `YYSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOBBBBBBBBBBSSSSSOOOOOOO`
- Cols 7-8: YY
- Cols 9-13: SSSSS (left square)
- Cols 14-28: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBB (15 cells)
- Cols 29-31: YYY
- Cols 32-33: OO
- Cols 34-43: BBBBBBBBBB (10 cells)
- Cols 44-47: SSSS (4 cells shown, but the square is 5x5, so it's cols 44-48)
- Cols 48-55: OOOOOOOO
So the right square is at cols 44-48, which is the same as before. But the left square moved from cols 14-18 to cols 9-13.
Wait, but the animation shows 50 cells changed. If only the left square moved, it would be 25 cells. If both moved, it would be 50 cells. So both squares must have moved.
But the right square is at cols 44-48, which is the same as before. Let me check if the right square moved vertically instead.
Actually, looking at the boundary: `[[54, 44], [54, 48], [58, 48], [58, 44]]`. This is the same as before (row 54, cols 44-48). So the right square didn't move.
But the animation shows 50 cells changed. This is confusing. Maybe the animation includes the "old" positions being cleared (25 cells) and the "new" positions being set (25 cells), totaling 50 cells. But if only the left square moved, that would be 25 old + 25 new = 50 cells.
OK, so the left square moved from cols 14-18 to cols 9-13, and the right square stayed at cols 44-48. This means the right square is blocked from moving further right.
Now the left square is at col 9, which is near the left edge of the shape. Let me check if it can move further left.
Actually, I notice something interesting. The left square is now at cols 9-13, and to the left of it are YY cells (cols 7-8). So the left square is partially overlapping with the yellow region. This is similar to how the right square extends beyond the shape's boundary at the bottom.
Let me think about the goal. The squares can move vertically (UP/DOWN) and horizontally (LEFT/RIGHT). The squares can move independently when one is blocked. The squares can extend beyond the shape's boundary.
Maybe the goal is to move the squares to specific positions where they "match" the shape's pattern. For example:
- Left square at the left edge (col 9) and right square at the right edge (col 44)
- Both squares at the bottom (row 54)
Currently, the left square is at col 9 and the right square is at col 44. Both are at row 54. This seems like a "corner" configuration.
Let me try pressing SPACE to see if this configuration completes the level.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Left square moved to col 9 (overlapping yellow), right square blocked at col 44. Both at row 54. Let me try SPACE to see if this configuration completes the level.
